specialism.name: base-show
specialism.task: Add concise Base.show(io::IO, ::MIME"text/plain", x::T) methods to Julia types whose default REPL representation would be unhelpful or overwhelming. Default Julia show dumps every field recursively; types that hold DataFrames, large dictionaries, nested arrays, or many scalar fields produce screens of text at the REPL. This specialism produces compact, informative custom show methods and accompanying unit tests so that interactive use of the package is pleasant without losing key summary information.

workflow.step: Enumerate concrete types defined in the package: grep -nrE '^(mutable )?struct ' src/ Exclude abstract types (abstract type ...) — they cannot be instantiated.

For each concrete type, classify its default show noisiness. A type is "noisy" if it holds at least one of: a DataFrame, a Dict with many entries, a large Array, another struct that is itself noisy, or more than ~6 fields. If the package already defines a Base.show method for a type (grep -nrE 'Base\.show' src/), skip that type.

For each noisy type with no existing show method, write:
function Base.show(io::IO, ::MIME"text/plain", x::T)
The method must print: - The type name (bare, without type parameters) on the first line. For parametric types such as `EnsembleKalmanProcess{FT,IT,P,...}`, print only `EnsembleKalmanProcess` — the type-parameter tuple adds noise without informing the reader about the instance's state. - 1–5 concise summary lines: key scalar fields (e.g. "process = Inversion()"), counts (e.g. "N_ensemble = 50"), iteration number, sizes of stored collections. Never print collection contents. - Total output: at most 10 lines.
Placement: adjacent to the type definition in the same source file (the existing convention — see `src/ParameterDistributions.jl:124–440`). Do not create a separate `src/show.jl` unless there is already one in the package.

Write a unit test for each new show method. The test must assert structural properties of the output (contains the bare type name, line count ≤ 10) rather than exact strings, so stylistic edits do not break the test. Use sprint(show, MIME("text/plain"), instance) to capture the output.
Placement: add the test as a `@testset "show"` block inside the matching per-module test file at `test/<Module>/runtests.jl` (e.g., a show test for `EnsembleKalmanProcess` goes in `test/EnsembleKalmanProcess/runtests.jl`). Do not create a standalone `test/show/` directory — that does not match the package's test-dispatch convention.
Verify: load the package (julia --project -e 'using <PackageName>') and run the test suite. Confirm the new tests pass and no pre-existing tests regress.

rubric.criterion: Every type classified as noisy in step 2 has a Base.show method after the task is applied.
show output is at most 10 lines for any instance, including edge cases (empty collections, zero-element structs, etc.).
The show method does not throw on any valid instance, including default-constructed or partially-populated instances.

Every data access inside a show method must be an O(1) metadata query: length(), size(), isempty(), first() on a lazy iterator, or direct field access. Never call collect(), sort(), filter(), keys()/values() followed by iteration, or any function that materialises a new collection from an existing one. This guarantees show is safe to call on structs holding millions of elements without triggering large allocations or hangs.
Unit tests assert structural properties (contains type name, line count), not exact strings. A cosmetic change to the show output does not break any test.
Pre-existing tests continue to pass; no unintended changes to other source files.

formatting.rule: First printed line: type name (and key size hints if cheap to compute), using print or println.
Subsequent lines indented by two spaces for readability.
Never print collection contents — only counts, sizes, or ranges.

Never allocate inside show: use length(), size(), isempty(), and first() on lazy iterators (e.g. values(dict)) rather than collect(), sort(), or any function that copies a collection. All data access must be O(1).
Use sprint(show, MIME("text/plain"), x) in tests to capture output without side effects.

Place the show method adjacent to the type definition in the same source file (matching the existing convention in `src/ParameterDistributions.jl`). Do not create a separate `src/show.jl` — there is no such file in this package.
formatting.example:
## Scenario: MyEnvironment holds a large Dict of DataFrames and a date range.
## Before (default Julia show — prints entire Dict contents)
julia> env MyEnvironment(data=Dict{String, DataFrame}(...dozens of rows...))
## After (custom show method)
function Base.show(io::IO, ::MIME"text/plain", x::MyEnvironment) println(io, "MyEnvironment") println(io, " n_basins : ", length(x.data)) println(io, " date_range: ", x.start_date, " to ", x.end_date) end
# julia> env # MyEnvironment # n_basins : 847 # date_range: 2000-01-01 to 2020-12-31
## Corresponding unit test
@testset "MyEnvironment show" begin env = MyEnvironment(...) # construct a minimal valid instance out = sprint(show, MIME("text/plain"), env) @test occursin("MyEnvironment", out) @test count(==('\n'), out) <= 10 end
